title: Async Runtimes: Tokio and Others
content:
Rust does not include a built-in async runtime. The standard library provides the Future trait and async/await syntax, but you need an external runtime to actually execute futures.

A runtime provides: an executor (polls futures to completion), a reactor (monitors I/O events), and task spawning (run multiple futures concurrently).

Tokio is the dominant runtime, used by most production Rust services. It provides a multi-threaded work-stealing executor, timers, I/O drivers, synchronization primitives, and channels.

The #[tokio::main] attribute macro transforms your main function into an async entry point, setting up the runtime automatically.
keyPoints: Rust has no built-in async runtime -- you choose one
Tokio is the most popular runtime with the largest ecosystem
#[tokio::main] sets up the runtime and makes main() async
Runtimes provide executors, I/O drivers, timers, and task spawning
Use current_thread flavor for simpler single-threaded scenarios
Libraries should be runtime-agnostic when possible
